subject.title: MealMind
subject.summary: AI meal-planning app that builds grocery lists from your fridge photos.

details.founders: Priya Shah (ex-Instacart PM), Tom Reyes (ex-DoorDash engineer)
details.traction: 6 weeks live; 1,200 signups, ~140 WAU, no revenue, 9% week-4 retention
details.market: Busy US households, framed as the $900B grocery market
details.business_model: Planned $7/mo subscription + grocery affiliate revenue (not charging yet)
details.the_ask: Raising on an $8M cap; wants help with grocery-chain partnerships

summary: Strong, relevant founders, but a thin and easily-copied product on top-down market framing, weak early retention, and a pitch that conflates three businesses. Not yet venture-ready.

jurors.name: Dana Okafor
jurors.rationale: Citing the entire $900B grocery market is top-down and unfocused. The real wedge — people who cook from what's in their fridge — is a thin, hard-to-monetize slice, and Mealime, Whisk, and ChatGPT already serve it for free. No articulated path from the wedge to a venture-scale market.

jurors.name: Marcus Lee
jurors.rationale: Strong, relevant operators: an ex-Instacart PM and an ex-DoorDash engineer is genuine founder-market fit for grocery. Both first-time founders, but the backgrounds are a real asset. This was the strongest part of the application.

jurors.name: Elena Vasquez
jurors.rationale: The fridge-photo-to-recipe loop is a thin feature, not a defensible product. It's directly replicable by ChatGPT with an image upload, and a foundation-model release could subsume the vision step overnight. No data moat or workflow lock-in described.

jurors.name: Sam Whitfield
jurors.rationale: The weakest signal. 9% week-4 retention on a consumer habit product is well below bar, 140 WAU after 6 weeks is light, and there's zero revenue with monetization still hypothetical. No evidence yet that users form a durable habit.

jurors.name: Aisha Bello
jurors.rationale: The pitch conflates three products (meal planner, grocery list, affiliate marketplace) without a crisp one-sentence wedge. The 'why now' is generic and the ask leads with a valuation cap rather than a sharp insight. Hard to tell in one read what the single most important thing is.
